sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=32, col=5).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 2, level 1.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 1 of about 17 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Let me analyze the color changes more carefully. The red agent and green target stayed in the same positions. The colored 4x4 blocks all changed color.
Let me look at the pattern of changes:
Left column (col 13):
- Before: w, S, p, g, w (rows 20, 26, 32, 38, 44)
- After: g, w, S, p, g
This looks like each color shifted DOWN by one position (with wraparound):
- w (row 44) -> row 20
- w (row 20) -> row 26... wait no, that doesn't work.
Let me look at it as a cycle:
Before: w(20), S(26), p(32), g(38), w(44)
After:  g(20), w(26), S(32), p(38), g(44)
So the sequence shifted: w,S,p,g,w -> g,w,S,p,g
This is a rightward shift (or downward shift) by 1 position with wraparound.
Right column (col 49):
Before: g(20), p(26), b(32), S(38), g(44)
After:  p(20), b(26), S(32), g(38), p(44)
Again, a shift by 1: g,p,b,S,g -> p,b,S,g,p
Top row (row 20, cols 13-49):
Before: w(13), g(19), S(25), b(31), p(37), Y(43), g(49)
After:  g(13), S(19), b(25), p(37), Y(43), g(49), p(49)
Wait, col 49 is shared with the right column. Let me separate the top row from the columns.
Top row (excluding col 13 and col 49): cols 19, 25, 31, 37, 43
Before: g(19), S(25), b(31), p(37), Y(43)
After:  S(19), b(25), p(37), Y(43), g(43)
This is also a shift by 1! g,S,b,p,Y -> S,b,p,Y,g
Bottom row (row 44, cols 13-49):
Before: w(13), w(19), b(25), b(31), S(37), p(43), g(49)
After:  g(13), w(19), w(25), b(31), b(37), S(43), p(49)
Again, a shift by 1: w,w,b,b,S,p,g -> g,w,w,b,b,S,p
So clicking on the red agent causes ALL colored blocks to shift their colors by one position in a clockwise direction around the rectangular frame!
Wait, but the positions where blocks are located don't change - only their colors change. And the shift seems to follow the perimeter of the rectangle formed by the blocks.
Let me think about the frame as a loop:
Starting from top-left (20,13) and going clockwise:
- Top edge: (20,13), (20,19), (20,25), (20,31), (20,37), (20,43), (20,49)
- Right edge: (26,49), (32,49), (38,49), (44,49)
- Bottom edge: (44,43), (44,37), (44,31), (44,25), (44,19), (44,13)
- Left edge: (38,13), (32,13), (26,13)
That's 7 + 4 + 6 + 3 = 20 positions.
Let me check if the colors shift along this loop:
Before colors in clockwise order:
w, g, S, b, p, Y, g, p, b, S, g, g, p, S, b, b, w, w, g, p
After colors:
g, S, b, p, Y, g, p, b, S, g, p, g, w, S, b, b, w, w, p, g
Hmm, let me check if each color moved one position clockwise:
Position 0 (20,13): w -> g (was at position 18?)
Position 1 (20,19): g -> S (was at position 2?)
Position 2 (20,25): S -> b (was at position 3?)
Wait, let me check: if each color moves one position clockwise, then position i gets the color from position i-1.
Position 0 gets color from position 19 (last): p -> but we got g. That doesn't match.
Let me try counterclockwise:
Position 0 gets color from position 1: g -> g. That matches!
Position 1 gets color from position 2: S -> S. That matches!
Position 2 gets color from position 3: b -> b. That matches!
So the colors shift counterclockwise! Each position gets the color from the next position in clockwise order.
Wait, let me re-check:
Before: w, g, S, b, p, Y, g, p, b, S, g, g, p, S, b, b, w, w, g, p
After:  g, S, b, p, Y, g, p, b, S, g, p, g, w, S, b, b, w, w, p, g
Position 0: w -> g (from position 1: g) ✓
Position 1: g -> S (from position 2: S) ✓
Position 2: S -> b (from position 3: b) ✓
Position 3: b -> p (from position 4: p) ✓
Position 4: p -> Y (from position 5: Y) ✓
Position 5: Y -> g (from position 6: g) ✓
Position 6: g -> p (from position 7: p) ✓
Position 7: p -> b (from position 8: b) ✓
Position 8: b -> S (from position 9: S) ✓
Position 9: S -> g (from position 10: g) ✓
Position 10: g -> p (from position 11: g)... wait, position 11 is g, not p.
Hmm, let me re-examine. Let me list the positions more carefully.
Actually wait, I need to be more careful about the order. Let me list all 20 block positions in clockwise order around the frame:
Top edge (left to right): (20,13), (20,19), (20,25), (20,31), (20,37), (20,43), (20,49)
Right edge (top to bottom): (26,49), (32,49), (38,49), (44,49)
Bottom edge (right to left): (44,43), (44,37), (44,31), (44,25), (44,19), (44,13)
Left edge (bottom to top): (38,13), (32,13), (26,13)
Before colors:
(20,13)=w, (20,19)=g, (20,25)=S, (20,31)=b, (20,37)=p, (20,43)=Y, (20,49)=g
(26,49)=p, (32,49)=b, (38,49)=S, (44,49)=g
(44,43)=p, (44,37)=S, (44,31)=b, (44,25)=b, (44,19)=w, (44,13)=w
(38,13)=g, (32,13)=p, (26,13)=S
After colors:

(26,49)=b, (32,49)=S, (38,49)=g, (44,49)=p
(44,43)=S, (44,37)=b, (44,31)=b, (44,25)=w, (44,19)=w, (44,13)=g
(38,13)=p, (32,13)=S, (26,13)=w
So the sequence before is:
w, g, S, b, p, Y, g, p, b, S, g, p, S, b, b, w, w, g, p, S
And after:
g, S, b, p, Y, g, p, b, S, g, p, S, b, b, w, w, g, p, S, w
This is a shift by 1 position in the clockwise direction! Each color moves one step clockwise.

...

Position 18 (38,13): before=g, after=p
Position 19 (26,13): before=S, after=w
If counterclockwise shift: position 18 gets from position 19: S -> but we got p. Doesn't match.
Let me try clockwise shift:
Position 0 gets from position 19: S -> but we got g. Doesn't match.
Hmm, let me try a different ordering. Maybe the loop goes differently.
Actually, let me reconsider. Maybe the loop is:
Top edge (left to right): (20,13), (20,19), (20,25), (20,31), (20,37), (20,43), (20,49)
Right edge (top to bottom): (26,49), (32,49), (38,49), (44,49)
Bottom edge (right to left): (44,43), (44,37), (44,31), (44,25), (44,19), (44,13)
Left edge (bottom to top): (38,13), (32,13), (26,13)
Before: w, g, S, b, p, Y, g, p, b, S, g, p, S, b, b, w, w, g, p, S
After:  g, S, b, p, Y, g, p, b, S, g, p, S, b, b, w, w, g, p, S, w
Let me check if this is a simple rotation:
After[0] = g = Before[1] ✓
After[1] = S = Before[2] ✓
After[2] = b = Before[3] ✓
After[3] = p = Before[4] ✓
After[4] = Y = Before[5] ✓
After[5] = g = Before[6] ✓
After[6] = p = Before[7] ✓
After[7] = b = Before[8] ✓
After[8] = S = Before[9] ✓
After[9] = g = Before[10] ✓
After[10] = p = Before[11] ✓
After[11] = S = Before[12] ✓
After[12] = b = Before[13] ✓
After[13] = b = Before[14] ✓
After[14] = w = Before[15] ✓
After[15] = w = Before[16] ✓
After[16] = g = Before[17] ✓
After[17] = p = Before[18] ✓
After[18] = S = Before[19] ✓
After[19] = w = Before[0] ✓
Yes! This is a rotation by 1 position. Each color moves one position in the direction of the loop (clockwise around the frame).
So clicking the red agent rotates all colors one step clockwise around the rectangular frame.
Now, what's the goal? The red agent is on the left and the green target is on the right. Maybe the goal is to get all colors to match in some way, or to get specific colors to specific positions.
Looking at the bottom row:
Before: w, w, b, b, S, p, g
After: g, w, w, b, b, S, p
And the top row:
Before: w, g, S, b, p, Y, g
After: g, S, b, p, Y, g, p
The yellow block (Y) is at position (20,43) and it's the only yellow block. It moved to (20,49) after the click.
Wait, I also noticed there are some special elements:
- Yellow 2x2 blocks at (17,10), (17,16), (23,10), (23,16) - these seem to be markers
- Charcoal 2x4 blocks at (20,16) and (23,13) - these seem to be gaps or special elements
Let me think about what the goal might be:
1. Maybe I need to rotate the colors until they match some pattern
2. Maybe I need to get specific colors to specific positions
3. Maybe the red agent needs to "reach" the green target by having matching colors
Let me look at the yellow markers more carefully. They're at:
- (17,10), (17,16) - above the top-left corner
- (23,10), (23,16) - below the top-left corner, around the charcoal gap
These might indicate the "start" position or some reference point.
Actually, looking at the layout again, the yellow 2x2 blocks form a bracket around the top-left area. The charcoal blocks at (20,16) and (23,13) are gaps in the frame.
Hmm, let me think about this differently. The frame has 20 colored blocks arranged in a loop. Clicking the red agent rotates all colors by 1. Maybe I need to rotate until the colors form a symmetric pattern or match some target configuration.
Let me look at the initial configuration more carefully:
Top: w, g, S, b, p, Y, g
Right: p, b, S, g
Bottom: p, S, b, b, w, w
Left: g, p, S
Is there a pattern here? The top and bottom rows have 7 and 6 blocks respectively, while the left and right columns have 5 and 4 blocks.
Actually, I think the goal might be to make the left and right columns match, or to make the top and bottom rows match, or to create some symmetric pattern.
Let me try clicking again to see the next rotation and look for patterns.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the problem. The user said there are about 17 target actions. If each click rotates by 1, and there are 20 positions, then maybe I need to click about 17 times to get to the right configuration.
But that seems like a lot. Let me think about what the goal configuration might be.

- w (light gray): appears 4 times
- g (gray): appears 4 times

- Total: 21... but we have 20 positions
Let me recount from the initial state:
Top row: w, g, S, b, p, Y, g (7 blocks)
Right col: p, b, S, g (4 blocks, excluding corner)
Bottom row: p, S, b, b, w, w (6 blocks, excluding corner)
Left col: g, p, S (3 blocks, excluding corner)
Total: 7 + 4 + 6 + 3 = 20
Colors:
w: 1 (top) + 2 (bottom) = 3
g: 2 (top) + 1 (right) + 1 (left) = 4
S: 1 (top) + 1 (right) + 1 (bottom) + 1 (left) = 4
b: 1 (top) + 1 (right) + 2 (bottom) = 4
p: 1 (top) + 1 (right) + 1 (bottom) + 1 (left) = 4
Y: 1 (top) = 1
Total: 3 + 4 + 4 + 4 + 4 + 1 = 20 ✓
So we have 3 w, 4 g, 4 S, 4 b, 4 p, 1 Y.
Hmm, the colors are mostly balanced except for w (3) and Y (1). 
Let me think about what the goal might be. Maybe I need to get the Y block to a specific position, or to create a symmetric pattern.
Actually, let me look at the charcoal gaps. They're at (20,16) and (23,13). These are between the top-left corner and the rest of the frame. Maybe they indicate where the "start" of the loop is.
Let me try a different approach: click a few more times and see if a pattern emerges that indicates the goal.
Actually, I just realized something. The yellow 2x2 blocks at (17,10), (17,16), (23,10), (23,16) form a kind of bracket. They might indicate that the top-left area is special. The charcoal blocks at (20,16) and (23,13) might be "gaps" where colors should NOT be.
Wait, but the charcoal blocks are not part of the 20 colored blocks. They're separate elements. So the frame is actually:
- Top row: 7 blocks at cols 13, 19, 25, 31, 37, 43, 49
- Right col: 4 blocks at rows 26, 32, 38, 49 (but row 49 is shared with bottom)
- Bottom row: 6 blocks at cols 43, 37, 31, 25, 19, 13 (but col 13 is shared with left)
- Left col: 3 blocks at rows 38, 32, 26 (but row 26 is shared with top)
Hmm, the corners are shared. Let me recount:
- Top-left corner: (20,13) - shared by top and left
- Top-right corner: (20,49) - shared by top and right
- Bottom-right corner: (44,49) - shared by right and bottom
- Bottom-left corner: (44,13) - shared by bottom and left
So the loop is:
Top: (20,13), (20,19), (20,25), (20,31), (20,37), (20,43), (20,49) - 7 blocks
Right (excluding corners): (26,49), (32,49), (38,49) - 3 blocks
Bottom (excluding corners): (44,43), (44,37), (44,31), (44,25), (44,19) - 5 blocks
Left (excluding corners): (38,13), (32,13), (26,13) - 3 blocks
Total: 7 + 3 + 5 + 3 = 18... but we have 20 blocks.
Wait, I need to include all corners:
Top: (20,13), (20,19), (20,25), (20,31), (20,37), (20,43), (20,49) - 7 blocks
Right (excluding top-right corner): (26,49), (32,49), (38,49), (44,49) - 4 blocks
Bottom (excluding bottom-right corner): (44,43), (44,37), (44,31), (44,25), (44,19), (44,13) - 6 blocks
Left (excluding bottom-left and top-left corners): (38,13), (32,13), (26,13) - 3 blocks
Total: 7 + 4 + 6 + 3 = 20 ✓
OK so the loop is 20 blocks. Clicking rotates all colors by 1 position clockwise.
Now, what's the goal? Let me think about this more carefully.
Looking at the initial configuration:
Position 0 (20,13): w
Position 1 (20,19): g

The green target is at (32, 59) which is to the right of position 8 (32,49).
Maybe the goal is to have the colors at positions 8 and 18 match (both on the same row as the agent/target)? Or maybe the goal is to have the colors at positions 8 and 18 be the same as the agent and target colors?
The agent is red (R) and the target is light green (N). Neither of these colors appears in the frame. So that's probably not it.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal is to create a symmetric pattern. For example, if the left and right columns mirror each other, or if the top and bottom rows mirror each other.
Left column (top to bottom): w, S, p, g, w
Right column (top to bottom): g, p, b, S, g
For symmetry, we'd want:
Left: X, Y, Z, Y, X (palindrome)
Right: X, Y, Z, Y, X (same palindrome)
Or maybe:
Left: A, B, C, D, E
Right: E, D, C, B, A (mirror)
Currently:
Left: w, S, p, g, w
Right: g, p, b, S, g
The left column is already a palindrome: w, S, p, g, w (wait, that's not a palindrome: w-S-p-g-w, reversed is w-g-p-S-w, which is different).
Hmm, let me reconsider. Maybe the goal is to make the left and right columns identical?
Left: w, S, p, g, w
Right: g, p, b, S, g
For them to be identical, we'd need to rotate until:
Left = Right
Since each rotation shifts all colors by 1, the relative positions between left and right columns stay the same. So the left and right columns can never become identical through rotation alone.
Wait, that's not right. The colors are rotating around the loop, so the colors at each position change. Let me think about this more carefully.
After k rotations, the color at position i becomes the color that was at position (i-k) mod 20.
For the left and right columns to match:
Left positions: 0, 19, 18, 17, 16
Right positions: 6, 7, 8, 9, 10
For them to match after k rotations:
Color at 0 after k = Color at 6 after k
=> Color at (0-k) mod 20 = Color at (6-k) mod 20
=> Color at (-k) mod 20 = Color at (6-k) mod 20
This means we need the colors at positions (-k) and (6-k) to be the same in the original configuration. The difference between these positions is 6.
So we need: color[i] == color[i+6] for all i in the relevant range.
Let me check:
color[0] = w, color[6] = g -> not equal
color[19] = S, color[5] = Y -> not equal
...
This doesn't seem to work for any k.
Let me try a different approach. Maybe the goal is to get specific colors to specific positions. For example, maybe the Y (yellow) block needs to be at a specific position.
Or maybe the goal is simpler: just click until the level completes. Let me try clicking a few more times and see if anything special happens.
Actually, let me re-examine the problem. The user said there are about 17 target actions. If each click rotates by 1, and there are 20 positions, then 17 clicks would rotate by 17 positions, which is equivalent to rotating by -3 positions (or 3 positions counterclockwise).
Let me check what the configuration looks like after 17 rotations:
After 17 rotations, position i has the color from position (i-17) mod 20 = (i+3) mod 20.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The click on red agent rotated all colors clockwise by 1 position around the frame loop.
# Let me verify by clicking again and checking the rotation.

# First, let me record current state
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']

current_blocks = {}
for n in nodes:
    if n['pixels'] == 16:
        cr = sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        cc = sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        current_blocks[(cr, cc)] = n['color']

# Define the loop order (clockwise)
loop_positions = [
    (20,13), (20,19), (20,25), (20,31), (20,37), (20,43), (20,49),  # top
    (26,49), (32,49), (38,49), (44,49),  # right
    (44,43), (44,37), (44,31), (44,25), (44,19), (44,13),  # bottom
    (38,13), (32,13), (26,13),  # left
]

current_colors = [current_blocks.get(pos, '?') for pos in loop_positions]
print(f"Current colors in loop order: {current_colors}")

# Click on red agent again
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 32, 'col': 5}])
print(f"\nClicked red agent again. Result: {last_action_result.get('board_changed', 'unknown')}")

# Check new state
seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
new_blocks = {}
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] == 16:
        cr = sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        cc = sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        new_blocks[(cr, cc)] = n['color']

new_colors = [new_blocks.get(pos, '?') for pos in loop_positions]
print(f"New colors in loop order: {new_colors}")

# Verify rotation
print(f"\nRotation check:")
for i in range(20):
    expected = current_colors[i]  # After clockwise rotation, pos i gets color from pos (i+1)%20... wait
    # Actually, clockwise rotation means each color moves to the next position
    # So new_colors[i] should equal current_colors[(i-1)%20]
    expected = current_colors[(i-1) % 20]
    match = "✓" if new_colors[i] == expected else "✗"
    if new_colors[i] != expected:
        print(f"  Pos {i}: expected {expected}, got {new_colors[i]} {match}")

print("All match!" if all(new_colors[i] == current_colors[(i-1)%20] for i in range(20)) else "Some mismatches")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

New colors in loop order: ['S', 'b', 'p', 'Y', 'g', 'p', 'b', 'S', 'g', 'p', 'S', 'b', 'b', 'w', 'w', 'g', 'p', 'S', 'w', 'g']

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=32, col=5).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 2, level 1.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 1 of about 17 target actions so far.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The click on red agent rotated all colors clockwise by 1 position around the frame loop.
# Let me verify by clicking again and checking the rotation.

# First, let me record current state
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']

current_blocks = {}
for n in nodes:
    if n['pixels'] == 16:
        cr = sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        cc = sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        current_blocks[(cr, cc)] = n['color']

# Define the loop order (clockwise)
loop_positions = [
    (20,13), (20,19), (20,25), (20,31), (20,37), (20,43), (20,49),  # top
    (26,49), (32,49), (38,49), (44,49),  # right
    (44,43), (44,37), (44,31), (44,25), (44,19), (44,13),  # bottom
    (38,13), (32,13), (26,13),  # left
]

current_colors = [current_blocks.get(pos, '?') for pos in loop_positions]
print(f"Current colors in loop order: {current_colors}")

# Click on red agent again
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 32, 'col': 5}])
print(f"\nClicked red agent again. Result: {last_action_result.get('board_changed', 'unknown')}")

# Check new state
seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
new_blocks = {}
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] == 16:
        cr = sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        cc = sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        new_blocks[(cr, cc)] = n['color']

new_colors = [new_blocks.get(pos, '?') for pos in loop_positions]
print(f"New colors in loop order: {new_colors}")

# Verify rotation
print(f"\nRotation check:")
for i in range(20):
    expected = current_colors[i]  # After clockwise rotation, pos i gets color from pos (i+1)%20... wait
    # Actually, clockwise rotation means each color moves to the next position
    # So new_colors[i] should equal current_colors[(i-1)%20]
    expected = current_colors[(i-1) % 20]
    match = "✓" if new_colors[i] == expected else "✗"
    if new_colors[i] != expected:
        print(f"  Pos {i}: expected {expected}, got {new_colors[i]} {match}")

print("All match!" if all(new_colors[i] == current_colors[(i-1)%20] for i in range(20)) else "Some mismatches")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
